We welcome its intent to strengthen the public service principles, especially political neutrality, which we recognize as to a well-functioning and integrous public service.

The Public Service Commission has described political neutrality as a hallmark of New Zealand system of government. It enables the public service to serve any government of the day, as well as New Zealanders fairly and professionally. When neutrality is strong, citizens can trust the state. But when neutrality erodes, trust follows, unfortunately, we believe that we are witnessing this erosion in real time.

The commission's own long-term insights briefing paper entitled The Future of Public Service Integrity, recently identified politicization as a concern in the public service. Politicization is necessarily a warning signal of weakening political neutrality. Local analysis cited in the briefing identified politically responsive behavior by public servants as a more subtle form of politicization in the public service.

These findings are consistent with concerns that the Free Speech Union has been attempting to raise throughout this year, following concerns that we've heard from members, supporters, and public servants across a number of agencies. In practice, we see political neutrality as being tested in two ways. First, by superiors managers, leaders, or senior officials, or in professional development sessions who make overtly p political statements, setting a tone that implies that certain views are acceptable and that others are not.

Second, by a growing sense, that staying silent is the safest course of action. Many public servants, often those whose views reflect White New Zealand society tell us that they self-censor, they keep their heads down, they don't speak freely for fear of formal or informal retribution from bosses and colleagues. The result is what social scientists call a false consensus.

When everyone assumes that everyone agrees because dissenting voices have gone quiet. And Wellington, especially this false consensus can make the public service appear more uniform than it really is, and frankly, less connected to the public that it serves. It also limits the ability to talk through policy ideas, risks, or issues with work to raise new ideas or discuss the limitations of existing dogma.

This quickly has the ability to affect the quality of work being delivered for the public, which will then further impact on public trust. We've also heard reports from about multiple public service leaders in public forums and statements, um, interpreting public service neutrality in a way that risks the will of the government of the day being undermined under the guise of continuity and consistency of service with time and without intervention.

This process reinforces a deepening spiral of silence for individual public servants, creates a real moral tension. Public servants who value neutrality and integrity now face three difficult choices, either conform and express support explicitly or through silence for the perceived prevailing views, even when it compromises their personal integrity and causes moral injury, or to speak out and risk professional and social consequences, or to opt out entirely and leave the public service.

We've spoken with public servants who are deeply committed to serving the New Zealand public, but feel increasingly trapped between their conscience and a career that they value highly. This is surely not the kind of environment that we want for our public servants. It's worth noting that this will not be felt so acutely or possibly even at all by public servants who share the perceived majority view.

This is the kind of experience that only becomes visible when individuals find themselves with an alternative perspective than the perceived majority. True neutrality requires an environment of team psychological safety that is a shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk taking. Public servants need to believe that it's safe to speak honestly, to take risks, and to raise uncomfortable or contentious ideas while the Public Service commission's speaking out process exists.

In theory, its effectiveness depends on trust, trust that concerns that will be taken seriously addressed adequately, and not result in adverse repercussions. The most recent public service census shows that this trust is lacking, and that finding is entirely consistent with what we hear from those inside the system.

The situation will not improve itself, and in fact, without intervention is likely to only get worse. We therefore welcome this bill as an opportunity to renew commitment to the public service principles. As we noted in our submission, we encourage the committee to strengthen provisions for protecting free speech, including through speaking out processes and reinforcement of the rights enshrined by the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act to further support the intent of the bill being realized.

To this end, we again recommend more accountability on senior managers to ensure team psychological safety, including a robust culture of interpersonal risk taking and an acceptance of intellectual diversity throughout the public service. Ultimately, the public service principles, political neutrality, and an open environment for viewpoint and political dynamism need to be reinvigorated if the public services to remain credible and representative of all New Zealanders.
